items.q: What is the enhanced greenhouse effect?
items.correct: The strengthening of the natural greenhouse effect by human activities (mainly burning fossil fuels) which add CO2 and other gases to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The greenhouse effect is entirely man-made and harmful.
items.random_wrong: It is the hole in the ozone layer letting in more sun.
items.why: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — without it Earth would be -18 degrees C. The problem is enhancement. Students also confuse it with the (separate) ozone hole.

items.correct: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs — balancing economic, social and environmental goals.
items.misconception: Sustainable development means stopping all economic growth.
items.random_wrong: Sustainable development means using only solar power.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as anti-development. The Brundtland definition is about balance and intergenerational equity — not zero growth or one specific technology.

items.q: Why does the tropical rainforest have distinct vertical layers?
items.correct: Competition for light: emergents (40m+), canopy (capturing most light), understorey (shade-tolerant) and forest floor (sparse vegetation due to low light) — each adapted to its light level.
items.misconception: Rainforest layers exist because different animals live at different heights.
items.random_wrong: Rainforest layers exist because trees grow in age order tallest to shortest.
items.why: Light availability drives the stratification; animal niches are a consequence. Students reverse cause and effect — the structure is plant-competition-driven, with fauna adapting to existing layers.

items.q: What ocean temperature is needed for tropical storms?
items.why: It's 27 degrees. “20 degrees” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Saffir-Simpson. “Beaufort” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Australia 2019-20 wildfires burnt how many million hectares?
items.why: Exact answer: 18 million ha. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
